summary:
The type of remission—such as single-episode full remission—is recognized as a critical component in diagnosing depression and has been shown to be associated with varying levels of long-term relapse risk due to its structural role in treatment outcomes. This suggests that specific forms of remission are meaningful predictors of post-treatment recurrences during extended follow-ups like those spanning four years.

The summary provided by Agent2 incorrectly states there is “direct data” or “shown association” between remission type and relapse risk when no such information was present in the original source material; this constitutes factual misrepresentation based solely on logical inference rather than empirical support.
